---
title: "Bayesian Inference with MCMC"
subtitle: "Turing.jl Vignette 06"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
  gfm:
    toc: true
engine: julia
julia:
  project: ".."
---

## Introduction

This vignette demonstrates a complete Bayesian inference pipeline using [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org/) with an ODE model defined via [ModelingToolkit.jl](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/stable/). This mirrors the R odin2/dust2/monty workflow: define a model, generate synthetic data, specify priors, run MCMC, and analyse posteriors.

For a deterministic ODE model with Poisson observations, Turing.jl can embed the ODE solver directly inside the `@model`, letting the NUTS sampler explore the parameter space efficiently.

## Model and Data

First, define the deterministic SIR model and generate synthetic observations.

```{julia}
using ModelingToolkit
using ModelingToolkit: t_nounits as t, D_nounits as D
using DifferentialEquations
using Turing
using Distributions
using CairoMakie
using Random
using Statistics

@parameters β=0.5 γ=0.1 N_pop=1000.0
@variables S(t)=990.0 I(t)=10.0 R(t)=0.0

eqs = [
    D(S) ~ -β * S * I / N_pop,
    D(I) ~ β * S * I / N_pop - γ * I,
    D(R) ~ γ * I,
]

@named sir = ODESystem(eqs, t)
sir = structural_simplify(sir)
```

## Generate Synthetic Data

We simulate the deterministic model and add Poisson noise to the daily incidence.

```{julia}
prob_true = ODEProblem(sir, [], (0.0, 50.0), [sir.β => 0.5, sir.γ => 0.1])
sol_true = solve(prob_true, Tsit5(); saveat=1.0)

R_vals = sol_true[sir.R]
daily_incidence = max.(diff(R_vals), 1e-6)

Random.seed!(1)
observed = [rand(Poisson(λ)) for λ in daily_incidence]
data_times = sol_true.t[2:end]
println("Observed cases (first 10): ", observed[1:10])
```

## Set Up Inference

The Turing model embeds the ODE solver. For each proposed `(β, γ)`, we solve the ODE, compute daily incidence, and evaluate the Poisson likelihood.

```{julia}
sir_sys = sir  # capture for use in model closure

@model function sir_inference(observed, data_times)
    # Priors (matching R monty_dsl: Gamma with shape, scale parameterisation)
    β_est ~ Gamma(2, 1/4)      # shape=2, scale=1/4 → mean=0.5
    γ_est ~ Gamma(2, 1/20)     # shape=2, scale=1/20 → mean=0.1

    # Solve ODE
    prob = ODEProblem(sir_sys, [], (0.0, maximum(data_times)),
                      [sir_sys.β => β_est, sir_sys.γ => γ_est])
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(); saveat=data_times)

    if sol.retcode != ReturnCode.Success
        Turing.@addlogprob! -Inf
        return
    end

    # Daily incidence from ΔR
    R_vals = sol[sir_sys.R]
    R_at_0 = 0.0

    for i in eachindex(observed)
        R_prev = i == 1 ? R_at_0 : R_vals[i - 1]
        λ = max(R_vals[i] - R_prev, 1e-6)
        observed[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end

model = sir_inference(observed, collect(data_times))
```

## Run MCMC

We use the NUTS sampler — Turing.jl's default gradient-based sampler — which is more efficient than the random walk used in R monty.

```{julia}
chain = sample(model, NUTS(), 2000; progress=true)
```

## Results

```{julia}
β_samples = chain[:β_est][501:end]
γ_samples = chain[:γ_est][501:end]

println("β: mean = ", round(mean(β_samples); digits=3),
        ", 95% CI = [", round(quantile(vec(β_samples), 0.025); digits=3),
        ", ", round(quantile(vec(β_samples), 0.975); digits=3), "]")
println("γ: mean = ", round(mean(γ_samples); digits=3),
        ", 95% CI = [", round(quantile(vec(γ_samples), 0.025); digits=3),
        ", ", round(quantile(vec(γ_samples), 0.975); digits=3), "]")
println("True: β = 0.5, γ = 0.1")
```

```{julia}
fig = Figure(size=(700, 350))
ax1 = Axis(fig[1, 1]; xlabel="β", ylabel="Density", title="Posterior: β")
hist!(ax1, vec(β_samples); bins=30, normalization=:pdf, color=(:steelblue, 0.7))
vlines!(ax1, [0.5]; color=:red, linewidth=2)

ax2 = Axis(fig[1, 2]; xlabel="γ", ylabel="Density", title="Posterior: γ")
hist!(ax2, vec(γ_samples); bins=30, normalization=:pdf, color=(:steelblue, 0.7))
vlines!(ax2, [0.1]; color=:red, linewidth=2)
fig
```

## Trace Plots

```{julia}
fig2 = Figure(size=(700, 400))
ax1 = Axis(fig2[1, 1]; xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="β", title="Trace: β")
lines!(ax1, collect(1:length(vec(β_samples))), vec(β_samples); color=:steelblue, linewidth=0.5)
hlines!(ax1, [0.5]; color=:red, linewidth=2)

ax2 = Axis(fig2[2, 1]; xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="γ", title="Trace: γ")
lines!(ax2, collect(1:length(vec(γ_samples))), vec(γ_samples); color=:steelblue, linewidth=0.5)
hlines!(ax2, [0.1]; color=:red, linewidth=2)
fig2
```
